title: श्रीसूक्तम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री सूक्तम् ॥

ॐ हिरण्यवर्णां हरिणीं सुवर्णरजतस्रजाम्।
चन्द्रां हिरण्मयीं लक्ष्मीं जातवेदो म आवह॥ १॥

तां म आवह जातवेदो लक्ष्मीमनपगामिनीम्।
यस्यां हिरण्यं विन्देयं गामश्वं पुरुषानहम्॥ २॥

अश्वपूर्वां रथमध्यां हस्तिनादप्रबोधिनीम्।
श्रियं देवीमुपह्वये श्रीर्मा देवी जुषताम्॥ ३॥

कांसोस्मितां हिरण्यप्राकारामार्द्रां ज्वलन्तीं तृप्तां तर्पयन्तीम्।
पद्मे स्थितां पद्मवर्णां तामिहोपह्वये श्रियम्॥ ४॥

चन्द्रां प्रभासां यशसा ज्वलन्तीं श्रियं लोके देवजुष्टामुदाराम्।
तां पद्मिनीमीं शरणमहं प्रपद्येऽलक्ष्मीर्मे नश्यतां त्वां वृणे॥ ५॥

आदित्यवर्णे तपसोऽधिजातो वनस्पतिस्तव वृक्षोऽथ बिल्वः।
तस्य फलानि तपसा नुदन्तु माऽयान्तराया बाह्या अलक्ष्मीः॥ ६॥

उपैतु मां देवसखः कीर्तिश्च मणिना सह।
प्रादुर्भूतोऽस्मि राष्ट्रेऽस्मिन् कीर्तिमृद्धिं ददातु मे॥ ७॥

॥ इति श्री सूक्तम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
benefits: Sacred Sri Suktam for devotion and spiritual growth
Invokes blessings of Lakshmi in all forms
Traditionally recited for removal of sins and bestows spiritual merit
Ideal for Lakshmi festivals and daily worship
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Goddess Lakshmi, Sri Suktam (श्रीसूक्तम्) stands as a Vedic hymn. Rooted in Rig Veda Khilani, this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Devotees recite this prayer to grant divine protection and auspiciousness and invoke blessings of Lakshmi in all forms. It is also said to remove sins and bestow spiritual merit. For best results, devotees chant this on Friday (Shukravar) and during Diwali, Akshaya Tritiya, and Dhanteras. With 7 verses and a reading time of about 5 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Sri Suktam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Sri Suktam and where is it preserved in the Veda?
seoFaqs.answer: The Sri Suktam (श्रीसूक्तम्) is the supreme Vedic hymn to Devi Sri Lakshmi — the goddess of wealth, prosperity, beauty, fortune, abundance and grace. It is preserved in the Khila portion of the Rigveda — specifically as Rigveda Khila 2.6 (Khilani 5.87 in some numbering systems), an appendix-collection of hymns considered Vedic in authority and recited with Vedic svara accentuation. The Sri Suktam is the most ancient continuously-recited Lakshmi mantra in the Hindu tradition, predating all Puranic Lakshmi stotras by millennia. The full hymn comprises 15-16 mantras (the count varies between traditions, with some adding a phalashruti-like concluding mantra), each addressed to Sri Lakshmi by different aspects — Hiranya-varna (golden-coloured), Harini (the deer-like or yellow), Suvarna-rajata-sraja (with garlands of gold and silver), Padme-sthita (seated on a lotus), Padma-varna (lotus-coloured) and Ishana-varyanam (mistress of all desirables). (Sources: Rigveda Khila 2.6; classical Vedic recitation tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of the famous opening verse Hiranyavarnam Harinim?
seoFaqs.answer: The opening mantra — Hiranya-varnam Harinim Suvarna-rajata-srajam, Chandram Hiranmayim Lakshmim Jatavedo ma avaha — is the most chanted verse of the Sri Suktam. Word-by-word: Hiranya-varnam — golden-complexioned; Harinim — deer-like (graceful) or yellow-hued; Suvarna-rajata-srajam — wearing garlands woven of gold and silver; Chandram — moon-like (radiant, cool, beautiful); Hiranmayim — made of or wrought in gold; Lakshmim — the goddess Lakshmi (the auspicious, the lakshana-yukta, the bearer of all good signs); Jatavedah — O Agni, the all-knower, knower-of-all-births; ma avaha — bring her to me / invoke her for me. The devotee asks Agni — the Vedic priest of fire who carries oblations to the gods — to bring Devi Lakshmi to him in her supreme golden form. (Sources: Rigveda Khila 2.6 mantra 1; classical Vedic commentary.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Agni (Jatavedah) invoked at the start of the Sri Suktam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Sri Suktam invokes Agni in the form 'Jatavedah' (the knower of all births) because Agni is the principal devata of all Vedic yajna — the divine messenger and intermediary between earthly worshippers and celestial deities. In the Vedic worldview no offering reaches a deity without being carried through Agni (havya-vahana, the conveyor of oblations). The Sri Suktam is therefore structured as a yajna-context invocation in which the devotee, having lit Agni and placed oblations into the fire, formally requests Agni to summon Devi Sri Lakshmi to receive worship and to come and dwell with the devotee. Two key mantras (1 and 2) explicitly address Agni-Jatavedah with 'ma avaha' (bring her to me) and 'tam ma avaha Jatavedo Lakshmim-anapagaminim' (bring her to me — Lakshmi who never departs). The Sri Suktam is thus traditionally recited together with Sri Sukta Homa where each verse is followed by a fire-offering. (Sources: Sri Suktam mantras 1-2; classical Vedic yajna paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the Sri Sukta Homa and when is it performed?
seoFaqs.answer: The Sri Sukta Homa is the formal Vedic fire-ritual in which the Sri Suktam is chanted with each mantra accompanied by an oblation of ghee, payasa, lotus seeds, bilva leaves, panchamrita, navaratna, rice or other prescribed items into the consecrated yajna-kunda. Performed by qualified Vedic priests, the homa is one of the most powerful classical remedies for poverty, debt, business failure, marriage delays and the affliction of weak Venus or weak Jupiter in a chart. The most auspicious occasions are: Diwali / Lakshmi Pujan (Kartik Krishna Amavasya), Dhanteras (Kartik Krishna Trayodashi — the day of Dhanvantari and Kubera), Akshaya Tritiya (Vaisakha Shukla Tritiya — Lakshmi-Kubera ascendancy), Varalakshmi Vratam (Shravan Shukla Friday before Purnima), every Friday (Shukravar — Venus and Lakshmi day), every Purnima, Sharad Purnima, Mahalaya Amavasya and Navaratri. (Sources: classical Sri Sukta Homa paddhati; Padma Purana.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the benefits of regular Sri Suktam recitation?
seoFaqs.answer: Traditional Vedic teachers and the classical Sri Sukta phalashruti attribute these benefits to regular Sri Suktam recitation: arrival and permanent dwelling of Mata Lakshmi in the home (anapagamini — she who never departs); destruction of Alakshmi (the goddess of inauspiciousness, debt, misfortune); abundance of dhana (wealth), dhanya (grain), gomata (cows), ashva (horses/vehicles) and putra (children/lineage); kirti (fame) and riddhi (prosperity) in the world; removal of debts and financial distress; success in business and career; restoration of health and family harmony (since material distress often disturbs both); planetary remedy for weak Venus (Shukra) and weak Jupiter (Guru) in the natal chart; and the supreme phala — the inner Lakshmi (Antar-Lakshmi) of contentment, devotion and grace. Recitation of 11 or 108 times daily for 41 days is a classical purascharana for rapid results. (Sources: Sri Sukta phalashruti; Padma Purana; Lakshmi Tantra.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the difference between Sri Suktam and Lakshmi Ashtottara Shatanamavali?
seoFaqs.answer: Both are powerful Lakshmi prayers but come from different textual streams and serve different ritual functions. The Sri Suktam is a Vedic hymn from the Rigveda Khila — recited with Vedic svara accentuation, structured as an Agni-Jatavedah invocation in 15-16 mantras, and forming the core mantra-set for the formal Vedic Sri Sukta Homa. It is the most ancient and supreme authority-prayer for Lakshmi worship across all Vedic and Puranic Hindu traditions. The Lakshmi Ashtottara Shatanamavali is a Puranic-tradition enumeration of 108 names of Devi Lakshmi (Adi Lakshmi, Dhana Lakshmi, Vidya Lakshmi etc.), used principally for archana — the ritual offering of flowers, akshat or kumkum to a Lakshmi murti or yantra with one item per name. Tradition recommends both: Sri Suktam for the homa and main parayana, Ashtottara for the personal puja archana. The Lakshmi Sahasranama (1000 names) is the most expansive form. (Sources: Rigveda Khila; Padma Purana; classical Lakshmi puja paddhati.)
